author: @rhiantaylor3446
text: I recall in an O-level physics class discussing a chain hoist where the chain formed a continuous loop. The teacher explained that the efficiency was designed to be <50% which meant that however much the load was, the friction in the system was greater so the load would not fall back to the floor. I have always assumed that it was this effect that meant that a worm drive couldn't be driven in reverse.

author: @Forensic1Man
text: Robert!  We all do really enjoy watching and learning about all your projects -  they are all very good!  However, you have yet to really generate significant power that one can really use.  So far I have only seen low volts and milliamps of current.  I would like to see a project that generates some sizable energy to reduce my dependency on electricity and lower our utility bills.  Here in California the power companies really charge exorbitant amounts for KW/Hr.  Sooooo, what about designing a significant project that can supply real useful power for the home?

I feel like there is potential for a new planetary gear system using these worm gears.... I haven't quite figured it out yet, but it will have to expand into a more 3D system instead of the typical pancake arrangement....  maybe there is a way for the 'orbiting' gears to have worm gears stick out at an angle and driving... SOMETHING... 🤷‍♂ lol  - I don't see a use for any of this that the tradition planetary system can't already do 🤦‍♂  ... Once again I am building a solution for a non-existent problem LMAO 😂

author: @MK-lk7nc
text: Oh that's quite interesting, thanks for sharing.  Say, are you printing these gears in PLA?  That could be an issue, long term.  Have you tried nylon-blend filaments?  They're insanely strong and my tests indicate they wear and chip in gear applications far less than PLA.

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: Rob showed us that superglue coating trick .

replies.author: @MK-lk7nc
replies.text: @philip5940 yeah and that looks pretty great.  thing is I'm still pretty sure nylon is stronger and by a lot.  Worth a look.  I've actually had great success with weed trimmer line because for whatever reason, that magically fuses together as one solid blob with absolutely no weakness along grain lines.  there were no grainlines, it was all melted into one.  I made a pair of glasses frames out of it and you could tie them in a knot or hang from them on a pull up bar. tbh i think i could have towed a small car with them.  Nylon is crazy stuff.  i think it might not be as good for propellers tho, because it can flex a bit if used in thin sheets

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: my go to is PLA mate

replies.author: @MK-lk7nc
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering If you can get some of this sometime, "Taulman3D alloy 910", I think you'd rather like it.   

Don't get me wrong, PLA does a pretty great job,  but sometimes nylon is a better fit for my needs, especially when I need a part to be a strong and light as possible.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Oh i agree mate - but most of the things i print are prototypes meant to inspire others - so it is unlikely i will do much in nylon in the future - it's not that i am ignoring your suggestion - i appreciate it - specially as it is posted here as a hint and help to others - I just wanted to say that as i really do appreciate the help you give and i didn't want you thinking i was just ignoring you - cheers and thanks for the help

author: @msowdal
text: Neat gear, i hsve an off-subject question, Can aluminum wire be used as the coils in generators to save cost and weight?

replies.author: @philip5940
replies.text: The aluminium would have accelerated fatigue problems from my experience with it . Copper is pretty malleable. I wonder how well aluminium could be insulated by the enameling technique used for copper. Because of the lower conductivity of aluminium, you'd need more volume of it but it'd likely still be lighter. The lower conductivity is probably not a big deal at very high voltage low current levels though . It's been used in old tech arc welders successfully . It was tried for household wiring in late seventies in USA but it was responsible for an increase in house fires and discontinued . It works fine for electrical grid transmission lines .. I still can't believe that today we have plastic/aluminium radiators for new cars since way back.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ali is used but it is more resistive than copper so they tend to use thicker wire than copper to make up for that
